fileList.role: plugin-level human-readable description (root)
Apache-2.0 plugin license (root)
plugin manifest: name, description, author (Anthropic)
main skill entry point: capture intent, write skill, run evals, improve description, package
Apache-2.0 skill license
subagent: post-hoc analyzer (why winner won) + benchmark notes
subagent: blind A/B comparator of two outputs
subagent: grade expectations vs outputs, critique evals
static HTML template for human review of trigger-eval queries
stdlib-only HTTP server: serve embedded results, collect feedback.json
frontend HTML for the review page (embeds run data)
JSON schemas: evals.json, history.json, grading.json, metrics.json, timing.json, benchmark.json, comparison.json, analysis.json
package marker
aggregate per-run grading.json into benchmark.json/markdown with mean/stddev/delta
HTML report renderer for description-optimization loop
call `claude -p` to propose a new description; 1024-char hard-limit safety rewrite
zip a skill folder into a .skill file (excludes evals/, __pycache__, .DS_Store)
frontmatter validator: allowed keys, kebab-case name, <=1024 char description, no <>/, etc.
trigger eval: spawn `claude -p` per query, detect Skill/Read tool-use via stream-json
orchestrator: train/test split, run_eval + improve_description, return best by test score
parse_skill_md: extract (name, description, body) from frontmatter; multiline literal/folded

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: description: ...Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: description: ...Use when users want to create a Hermes skill from scratch, edit or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Reference is to 'Claude Code' / 'Claude.ai' skills; in Hermes the runtime is the Hermes agent and the skill format lives under ~/.hermes/skills. Frontmatter description is the primary triggering vector and must be Hermes-aware.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Create a few test prompts and run claude-with-access-to-the-skill on them
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Create a few test prompts and run hermes-with-access-to-the-skill on them
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Refers to spawning a 'Claude-with-skill' subagent. In Hermes the equivalent is launching a Hermes sub-session with the skill loaded into context.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Do NOT use `/skill-test` or any other testing skill.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Do NOT use `/skill-test` or any other testing skill (in Hermes, `/skill-test` is not registered; the eval loop is what we run here).
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Slash command /skill-test is Claude-Code-specific. In Hermes skills are invoked via the Skill tool/Hermes skill loader, not by a dedicated test slash command. The migration note must say 'if a user invokes /skill-test in Hermes, ignore it and continue with the local eval loop.'

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Step 1: Spawn all runs (with-skill AND baseline) in the same turn
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Step 1: Spawn all runs (with-skill AND baseline) in parallel in the same batch.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: 'Same turn' is a Claude-Code session concept. In Hermes the equivalent is dispatching all subagents in a single function-call batch so they execute concurrently; preserve the intent (parallel) but use neutral language.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: **With-skill run:** ... - Save outputs to: <workspace>/iteration-<N>/eval-<ID>/with_skill/outputs/
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: **With-skill run:** ... - Save outputs to: <workspace>/iteration-<N>/eval-<ID>/with_skill/outputs/ (Hermes convention: outputs/ is the materialized artifact directory)
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Path layout is portable. Hermes skills read/write files via the same filesystem tools; the convention is identical. Keep the layout, but document the path in the migration note.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: When each subagent task completes, you receive a notification containing `total_tokens` and `duration_ms`.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: When each subagent task completes, the task system returns a result record with `total_tokens` and `duration_ms` (or `total_duration_seconds`).
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Claude-Code exposes this in the task notification; Hermes returns it on the task result. Both fields are the same; update prose to remove the 'you receive a notification' framing.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: The description field in SKILL.md frontmatter is the primary mechanism that determines whether Claude invokes a skill.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: The description field in SKILL.md frontmatter is the primary mechanism that determines whether the Hermes agent invokes a skill.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace 'Claude' with 'the Hermes agent' to anchor description-triggering language to the new runtime.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Use the model ID from your system prompt (the one powering the current session) so the triggering test matches what the user actually experiences.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Use the model ID from the current Hermes session (the one powering the agent) so the triggering test matches what the user actually experiences.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: 'Your system prompt' is Claude-Code phrasing; the model is exposed by the Hermes harness via env/config. Update the lookup mechanism in the migration note.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Skills appear in Claude's `available_skills` list with their name + description, and Claude decides whether to consult a skill based on that description.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Skills appear in the Hermes agent's `available_skills` (or equivalent) list with their name + description, and the agent decides whether to consult a skill based on that description.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace 'Claude's' with 'the Hermes agent's'.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Check whether you have access to the `present_files` tool. If you don't, skip this step.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Check whether you have access to a file-presentation tool (`present_files` in Claude-Code, the equivalent in Hermes is a UI/share tool). If not, skip this step.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: `present_files` is Claude-Code-specific. Hermes has its own file-presentation surface (likely the Skill/UI tool); the gating check is still useful.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: ## Claude.ai-specific instructions
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: ## Hermes-runtime-specific instructions
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: This whole section is about differences between Claude-Code (subagents + CLI) and Claude.ai (no subagents, no CLI). For Hermes the analogous split is 'Hermes CLI/REPL mode' vs 'Hermes web/headless mode'. Replace prose; structure stays.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: In Claude.ai, the core workflow is the same... but because Claude.ai doesn't have subagents, some mechanics change.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: In Hermes headless/web mode the core workflow is the same... but if the runtime does not support subagents, some mechanics change.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Remove the Claude.ai-specific framing; describe the constraint (no subagents) generically.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: **Description optimization**: This section requires the `claude` CLI tool (specifically `claude -p`) which is only available in Claude Code. Skip it if you're on Claude.ai.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: **Description optimization**: This section requires the `hermes` CLI (specifically `hermes -p`) to be available on the host. Skip it on Hermes headless/web mode if the CLI is not present; in that case run the prompt via the configured model provider directly (see migration note §5.5 for the HTTP fallback).
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace `claude -p` with `hermes -p` (or a documented HTTP fallback for headless mode). The eval scripts (improve_description.py, run_eval.py) currently call `claude -p`; these are the highest-risk Claude-specific bindings (see claudeSpecificInvocations below).

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: ## Cowork-Specific Instructions
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: ## Hermes Headless/Server-Specific Instructions
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace Claude-Code Cowork mode with the Hermes analog (e.g. Hermes server/headless).

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: For whatever reason, the Cowork setup seems to disincline Claude from generating the eval viewer after running the tests...
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: For whatever reason, the Hermes headless setup may disincline the agent from generating the eval viewer after running the tests...
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace 'Cowork'/'Claude' with the Hermes analog.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: **Description optimization** (`run_loop.py` / `run_eval.py`) should work in Cowork just fine since it uses `claude -p` via subprocess, not a browser, but please save it until you've fully finished making the skill and the user agrees it's in good shape.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: **Description optimization** (`run_loop.py` / `run_eval.py`) should work in Hermes headless mode just fine since it uses `hermes -p` (or the HTTP fallback) via subprocess, not a browser, but please save it until you've fully finished making the skill and the user agrees it's in good shape.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: `claude -p` -> `hermes -p`; rest is host-agnostic.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: You are optimizing a skill description for a Claude Code skill called "{skill_name}". A "skill" is sort of like a prompt, but with progressive disclosure
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: You are optimizing a skill description for a Hermes skill called "{skill_name}". A "skill" is sort of like a prompt, but with progressive disclosure
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Inside the LLM prompt template; the host changes from 'Claude Code' to 'Hermes'.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: The description appears in Claude's "available_skills" list.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: The description appears in the Hermes agent's "available_skills" list.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace host reference in the LLM prompt.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: the description competes with other skills for Claude's attention
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: the description competes with other skills for the agent's attention
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace 'Claude's' with 'the agent's'.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Note: currently Claude has a tendency to "undertrigger" skills -- to not use them when they'd be useful.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Note: the Hermes agent has a tendency to "undertrigger" skills -- to not use them when they'd be useful.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace 'Claude' with 'Hermes agent'.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Claude reads only the relevant reference file.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: The Hermes agent reads only the relevant reference file.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace host reference.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: there's a trend now where the power of Claude is inspiring plumbers to open up their terminals, parents and grandparents to google "how to install npm"
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: there's a trend now where the power of AI agents is inspiring plumbers to open up their terminals, parents and grandparents to google "how to install npm"
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace the specific 'Claude' reference with a generic 'AI agents'. User-facing copy; the bilingual rule means a HU translation is needed.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: cmd = ["claude", "-p", "--output-format", "text"]
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: cmd = ["hermes", "-p", "--output-format", "text"]
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Subprocess invocation of the host CLI. In Hermes this is `hermes -p`.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: # Remove CLAUDECODE env var to allow nesting claude -p inside a Claude Code session.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: # Remove HERMES_SESSION and CLAUDECODE env vars to allow nesting `hermes -p` inside a Hermes session.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: The `CLAUDECODE` guard is Claude-Code's. Hermes has an equivalent nesting guard (likely `HERMES_SESSION` or `HERMES_AGENT`); the exact env-var name must be verified against the Hermes harness (the migration note must call this out as TBD).

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: env = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k != "CLAUDECODE"}
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: env = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k not in {"CLAUDECODE", "HERMES_SESSION"}}
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Same - keep the existing CLAUDECODE strip; add Hermes's nesting-guard var (TBD exact name; conservative default).

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: raise RuntimeError(f"claude -p exited {result.returncode}\nstderr: {result.stderr}")
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: raise RuntimeError(f"hermes -p exited {result.returncode}\nstderr: {result.stderr}")
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Error message references the new CLI.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Safety net: ... (The old SDK path did this as a true multi-turn; `claude -p` is one-shot, so we inline the prior output into the new prompt instead.)
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Safety net: ... (Hermes's `hermes -p` is also one-shot, so we inline the prior output into the new prompt instead.)
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Comment-only; rename `claude -p` -> `hermes -p`.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm:
def find_project_root() -> Path:
    """Find the project root by walking up from cwd looking for .claude/.
    Mimics how Claude Code discovers its project root, so the command file
    we create ends up where claude -p will look for it.
    """
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm:
def find_project_root() -> Path:
    """Find the project root by walking up from cwd looking for .hermes/ (and .claude/ as legacy).
    Mimics how the Hermes harness discovers its project root, so the command file
    we create ends up where `hermes -p` will look for it.
    """
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: The detection logic must look for both `.hermes/` and `.claude/` (legacy) project markers; this preserves the migration's back-compat story.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Creates a command file in .claude/commands/ so it appears in Claude's available_skills list, then runs `claude -p` with the raw query.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Creates a command file in .hermes/commands/ (or .claude/commands/ as legacy fallback) so it appears in the Hermes agent's available_skills list, then runs `hermes -p` with the raw query.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Staging directory must follow Hermes's plugin layout; keep Claude legacy for back-compat.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: Uses --include-partial-messages to detect triggering early from stream events (content_block_start)
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: Uses the host's stream-json / partial-events flag to detect triggering early from stream events
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Specific Claude-Code CLI flag; Hermes has an equivalent (TBD exact name - likely `--include-partial-messages` or `--stream-events`). Migration note must record this.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm:
cmd = [
    "claude",
    "-p", query,
    "--output-format", "stream-json",
    "--verbose",
    "--include-partial-messages",
]
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm:
cmd = [
    "hermes",
    "-p", query,
    "--output-format", "stream-json",
    "--include-partial-events",  # TBD: confirm Hermes flag name
]
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Replace the CLI and flags with the Hermes equivalents. The `claude`/`hermes` switch is high-risk and must be tested by Script #1 (patch script) and Script #2 (profile manager) before being called.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: # Remove CLAUDECODE env var to allow nesting claude -p inside a Claude Code session.
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: # Remove Hermes/Claude nesting-guard env vars (HERMES_SESSION, CLAUDECODE) so `hermes -p` can run inside a Hermes session.
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Same as improve_description.py.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: env = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k != "CLAUDECODE"}
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: env = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k not in {"CLAUDECODE", "HERMES_SESSION"}}
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Same.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: if tool_name in ("Skill", "Read"):
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: if tool_name in ("Skill", "Read", "skill", "read"):  # case-insensitive on Hermes; tool names are also case-insensitive per Hermes docs
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Claude-Code's tool names are PascalCase. Hermes's documented tool names (per Hermes plugin docs and the tool-name-mapping research) are lowercase (`skill`, `read`, `write`, `bash`, `edit`, `web_search`, `web_fetch`, ...). The migration must convert all tool-name references.
Same - normalize tool-name matching to lowercase per Hermes convention.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: .claude/commands/
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: .hermes/commands/
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Hermes skill/command path. Write to both for back-compat, prefer `.hermes/`.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: parser.add_argument("--model", required=True, help="Model for improvement")
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: parser.add_argument("--model", required=True, help="Model for improvement (Hermes model id)")
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Docstring only; the model id space differs between providers but the flag shape is identical.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: # Grader Agent
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: # Grader Subagent
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Hermes uses 'subagent' rather than 'agent' to avoid namespace collision with the primary agent.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: # Post-hoc Analyzer Agent
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: # Post-hoc Analyzer Subagent
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Same as grader.md - Hermes uses 'subagent'.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: # Blind Comparator Agent
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: # Blind Comparator Subagent
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Same.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: "description": "Create new skills, improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, or benchmark skill performance with variance analysis."
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: "description": "Create new Hermes skills, improve existing Hermes skills, and measure Hermes-skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, or benchmark skill performance with variance analysis."
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Plugin-level description must be Hermes-aware to trigger correctly.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: # skill-creator
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: # skill-creator (Hermes port)
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Top-level README is plugin metadata in Claude; in Hermes, the skill's own SKILL.md is the canonical description source. Keep the file for parity, but flag it as Claude-specific.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: ALLOWED_PROPERTIES = {'name', 'description', 'license', 'allowed-tools', 'metadata', 'compatibility'}
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: ALLOWED_PROPERTIES = {'name', 'description', 'license', 'allowed-tools', 'metadata', 'compatibility', 'model', 'when_to_use'}  # extend with Hermes-specific keys
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Hermes's frontmatter schema includes additional allowed keys (e.g. `model`, `when_to_use`) per the Hermes skill-authoring rules research. The migration must keep Claude's keys allowed (back-compat) and add Hermes-specific ones.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm:
if len(name) > 64:
    return False, f"Name is too long ({len(name)} characters). Maximum is 64 characters."
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: The 64-char cap is identical in Hermes; the migration should add a Hermes-specific collision check against the built-in skill registry.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm:
if len(description) > 1024:
    return False, f"Description is too long ({len(description)} characters). Maximum is 1024 characters."
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Same; add Hermes-specific word-count warning.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: """Shared utilities for skill-creator scripts."""
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: """Shared utilities for the Hermes-port of skill-creator."""
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Module docstring only.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm:
"""Run trigger evaluation for a skill description.

Tests whether a skill's description causes Claude to trigger (read the skill)
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm:
"""Run trigger evaluation for a skill description.

Tests whether a skill's description causes the Hermes agent to trigger (read the skill)
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Module docstring only.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: """Generate and serve a review page for eval results."""
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: (preserve)
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Host-agnostic; uses only stdlib.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: """Aggregate individual run results into benchmark summary statistics."""
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: (preserve)
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Host-agnostic.

claudeSpecificInvocations.claudeForm: """Generate an HTML report from run_loop.py output."""
claudeSpecificInvocations.proposedHermesForm: (preserve)
claudeSpecificInvocations.rationale: Host-agnostic.

claudeStrengthsToPreserve: Three-tier subagent specialization: grader (objectively verifiable assertions), analyzer (post-hoc why), comparator (blind A/B). The names should be normalized to 'subagent' but the role split is sound.</item>
evidence: Pinned upstream commit 2a40fd2e7c52207aa903bd33fc4c65716126966e ('skill-creator: sync from anthropics/skills (drop ANTHROPIC_API_KEY requirement) #1523') resolved via https://api.github.com/repos/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/commits?path=plugins/skill-creator&per_page=1. The local mirror at /Users/kiscsicska/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/plugins/skill-creator/ is byte-identical to the staging copy at /Users/kiscsicska/projects/easter-skills-hack-to-hermes-2/.claude/worktrees/hermes-skills-dev/research/anthropic-skill-creator-original/ (verified with diff -q on plugin.json, SKILL.md, utils.py, quick_validate.py - all returned no differences). File counts and sizes match the GitHub tree listing at the pinned commit. Total of 20 files: 1 README.md, 1 LICENSE, 1 .claude-plugin/plugin.json, 1 skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md, 1 skills/skill-creator/LICENSE.txt, 3 agents/{analyzer,comparator,grader}.md, 1 assets/eval_review.html, 2 eval-viewer/{generate_review.py,viewer.html}, 1 references/schemas.md, 9 scripts/{__init__,aggregate_benchmark,generate_report,improve_description,package_skill,quick_validate,run_eval,run_loop,utils}.py. Upstream commit message explicitly notes 'improve_description.py and run_loop.py now shell out to `claude -p` instead of using the Anthropic SDK directly' - the highest-risk Claude-specific surface. The 1024-char description cap and 64-char kebab-case name cap are preserved verbatim. Tool names in scripts/run_eval.py are PascalCase ('Skill', 'Read') and must be normalized to lowercase per Hermes convention. The `CLAUDECODE` env-var nesting guard must be augmented with Hermes's equivalent (TBD exact name - likely HERMES_SESSION or HERMES_AGENT; must be confirmed in §5.5 migration note). The eval_viewer/generate_review.py is host-agnostic (stdlib only) and ports unchanged.
